planned_changes.description: Open SLO_burn_1.xlsx in a spreadsheet runtime (Excel, LibreOffice, or Python with openpyxl) and validate: Model!B3, Model!B4, Model!E3, Model!C9:C11 values and formulas; sensitivity table entries; check for hidden sheets/cells and external links. Capture a short validation log (timestamp, runtime used, checks performed).
Add exact cell formula strings into expert_memo.md for core calculations (error_rate, burn_rate, remediation application formulas) and include references to exact workbook cell addresses.
Export and attach CSV snapshots of: (a) Packet rows used (R1,R2,R3), and (b) the full sensitivity table used for threshold sweep. Link these files from the memo for quick independent verification.
Include a worked example in the memo applying one remediation option to one record showing before/after numeric values (error_count, request_count, error_rate, burn_rate, budget impact).
Optionally enumerate feasible remediation combinations within budget (or explain why enumeration isn't provided). If enumerated, include a small table showing combination costs and feasibility flags.

Attach the validation log and the updated artifacts (updated expert_memo.md, CSV snapshots). Re-run any local checks and update the final note indicating that workbook runtime verification was completed.

metadata_json.revision_goal: Baseline deliverables are complete and internally consistent: expert_memo documents formulas, numeric calculations, sensitivity sweep results, remediation options and a conservative 'needs_review' classification because governing SLO and policy threshold are missing. The main limitation is that the xlsx could not be parsed in this environment (openpyxl unavailable), so the grader and verifier relied on the memo rather than in-spreadsheet verification. Recommend a short follow-up to validate workbook formulas, hidden cells, and external links in a spreadsheet runtime and to add a few supplemental artifacts to improve reproducibility and reviewer efficiency.
